canonical_claim: Any investment must be rejected unless the analyst can articulate in plain, first-principles terms how the business generates profit and why its competitive position is intelligible
Industries whose competitive dynamics require forecasting fundamental scientific breakthroughs or frontier technology pathways are out of scope
The target business must possess an identifiable, structurally self-reinforcing competitive barrier that protects its returns on capital
A structural competitive barrier that is demonstrably narrowing on a secular basis is disqualifying even if currently wide
The ability to raise prices in excess of input cost inflation without meaningful customer attrition is a primary behavioral test of whether a real structural competitive barrier exists
Sustained high returns on invested capital across a full business cycle are the primary quantitative signature of an operative structural barrier
The target's core brand should occupy the first-choice position in consumer mental association within its category
A high-quality business model should continue to operate profitably even under mediocre management
Any verified evidence of management dishonesty, deception, or lack of integrity unconditionally disqualifies the investment

Management's capital allocation must be oriented to long-term shareholder value, evidenced by retained earnings generating at least equivalent incremental market value rather than serving short-term or self-interested motives
Management evaluation must center on the incentive structure rather than stated character, reputation, or interview impressions

Management that demonstrably resists the institutional imperative — holding excess cash, declining available acquisitions, and defying peer behavior when economics do not justify action — constitutes a positive quality signal
The business model's structural incentives must align serving customers well with the path to maximum profitability
Dividends should be paid only when management cannot deploy retained capital at returns exceeding what owners could earn independently
A superior business generates cash substantially in excess of what is required to maintain its competitive position
The investment entity must not employ conventional borrowed capital, margin, or any leverage mechanism that creates exposure to forced liquidation, margin calls, or fixed maturity
For a high-conviction position, the default state is to hold indefinitely; selling requires affirmative, articulable justification
Price decline alone is not a legitimate sell trigger; only a substantive break in the investment thesis justifies exit
A buy decision must be psychologically conditioned on willingness to hold the position even if the market is closed for a long period
Upon recognition of a substantive thesis error, the analyst must immediately exit, regardless of current book loss on the position
The permanent compounding cost of realizing a capital gain must be explicitly weighed before any sell decision on a position with significant embedded unrealized gains
Business value must be assessed on distributable economic earnings rather than reported accounting earnings
An investment requiring a precise multi-year financial model to demonstrate cheapness lacks sufficient valuation buffer; formal DCF models must not serve as the primary source of valuation conviction
Full qualitative assessment of business quality, competitive durability, and culture must precede any evaluation of price attractiveness
The purchase price must imply an acceptable return even under a materially conservative downside earnings scenario, not only under the base case
A business with unfavorable long-term structural economics must be rejected regardless of apparent cheapness on near-term financial metrics
Before finalizing any investment decision, the analyst must explicitly audit the thesis for contamination by the framework's standard psychological bias taxonomy
Market consensus or the investment activity of peers must never be used as positive evidence supporting an investment decision
An investment thesis whose validity depends on a specific macroeconomic prediction or on market-timing judgment must not be relied upon as the core decision trigger
When a bull case depends on multiple independent assumptions holding simultaneously, the combined probability must be explicitly calculated rather than intuitively estimated
Post-mortem analysis of investment errors must identify the specific reasoning failure in the analyst's own judgment
Every investment opportunity must be evaluated against the explicit opportunity cost of the best alternative deployment of capital available at the time of decision
High-conviction investment opportunities are not bound by standard diversification constraints
